heading: libro della Sapienza203: Lo dissetò con l'acqua della sapienza salutare204.
card_summary: This chunk opens with Zorzi's meditation on the public and communicable nature of divine Wisdom, drawing on Proverbs/Sirach (Wisdom crying aloud in the streets and squares) and the Hermetic Corpus (the Krater passage from Poimandres, where God fills a great bowl with Nous/Wisdom and summons all who wish to receive it). Two reasons are given for Wisdom's public proclamation: universal accessibility and the special obligation of magistrates to be immersed in divine wisdom. Zorzi contrasts the celestial city's citizens — sustained by the Tree of Life in eternal beatitude — with those who absent themselves from Wisdom's fountain, invoking Jeremiah's Lamentations on spiritual desiccation. The chunk then pivots to a new section, "De mensura particulari secundum Ezechielem," initiating an extended Kabbalistic-typological analysis of Ezekiel's measuring reed (six cubits plus a handbreadth = 42 palms), connecting the prophetic vision's architectural measurements to divine proportionality and the mystery of the heavenly city.
essay:
## Central Theme

Chunk 106 bridges two distinct but related movements in Zorzi's *Cantico Terzo*: a concluding meditation on Wisdom's universal accessibility and the spiritual consequences of turning away from her, followed by the opening of a new and technically demanding section on the architectural measurements of Ezekiel's prophetic vision. The underlying thread is *mensura* — divine measure — which governs both the dispensation of Wisdom in the soul and the proportional structure of the heavenly city.

## Wisdom in the Squares: Universal Accessibility and the Hermetic Crater

Zorzi opens by invoking the image from Proverbs (and echoed in Sirach) of Wisdom crying out in the streets and public squares:

> vocem suam, in capite turbarum clamitat.

<cite>p. 2497 (Latin column)</cite>

He offers two reasons (*duplici de causa*) for this public proclamation. The first is that Wisdom is communicable to anyone willing to receive her. To support this, Zorzi reaches outside Scripture to the *Corpus Hermeticum*, specifically the famous Krater passage from *Poimandres* (Tractate IV), where Hermes Trismegistus speaks to his son Tat:

> «Dio riempì con la sapienza un grande cratere e mandò un araldo con l'ordine che vi si immergesse chiunque vuole ottenere la mente, che è la vera sapienza»

<cite>p. 2498 (Italian column, note 206)</cite>

The Latin text renders this as: *Deus craterem patulum sapientia implevit, et praeconem misit iubens, ut mergeret seipsum in hanc pateram, qui voluerit mentem, quae vera sapientia est, suscipere.* This is a characteristic Zorzian move: Hermetic texts are placed alongside Scripture as parallel witnesses to the same metaphysical truth. Nous (Mind/Intelligence) is equated with *vera sapientia*, the Wisdom that flows from God's own fountain.

The second reason for Wisdom's public proclamation is the special obligation of civic magistrates (*magistratibus, qui praecipue habitant in plateis*) to be immersed in divine wisdom. This carries a political-theological dimension: those who govern earthly spaces ought to reflect the ordering principles of the heavenly city.

## The Celestial City and the Tree of Life

Zorzi contrasts two classes of citizens: those of the *superior civitas* (the celestial Jerusalem) who possess the Tree of Life and partake continually of its fruits in *perpetua foelicitate*, and worldly citizens who, failing to frequent Wisdom's squares, wither spiritually:

> Il loro aspetto è più scuro del carbone, non si riconoscono più nelle piazze; la loro pelle è attaccata alle ossa, cioè si è seccata in mancanza del fiume di acqua viva, è diventata come legna secca destinata a bruciare nel fuoco eterno.

<cite>p. 2499 (Italian column, note 207)</cite>

The source is Jeremiah's *Lamentazioni* (Lam. 4:8 and 5:10), and Zorzi reads the physical desiccation described there as the spiritual consequence of separation from the *fons totius boni et pulchritudinis* — the Neoplatonic fountain of the Good and the Beautiful. The image of the "dry wood destined for eternal fire" inverts the life-giving imagery of the water of wisdom that heads the chunk.

## Transition to Ezekiel: De Mensura Particulari

The second half of the chunk introduces a major new subsection: *De mensura particulari secundum Ezechielem. Concentus I*. Zorzi signals that he has already treated the general dimensions of the heavenly city through both prophets (presumably Isaiah and the Apocalypse), but now turns to the more detailed and technically obscure measurements in Ezekiel 40–48.

He characterizes Ezekiel's style as deliberately difficult:

> is siquidem metitur suis calamis aedificium nescio quoddam satis obscure, item templum, gazophylatia, portas, vestibula, et multa quae ad illa aedificia conducibilia putat, postea torrentem, primitias, residua primitiarum, civitatem, et suburbia. Quae omnia tantis aenigmatibus involvit, et tam breviter exprimit, ut vix intelligi possit.

<cite>pp. 2499–2500 (Latin column)</cite>

Zorzi promises nonetheless to illuminate (*adumbrare*) these hidden mysteries, trusting that the same spirit who revealed them to the prophet will enlighten his own eyes (*nostros collustraverit oculos*).

## The Measuring Reed: 42 Palms

The technical core of this new section begins with Ezekiel 40:5, the measuring reed (*calamus mensurae*) of six cubits plus a handbreadth. Zorzi works through the arithmetic carefully:

- A cubit = 6 palms; add the extra handbreadth = 7 palms per cubit.
- Reed = 6 such cubits = 6 × 7 = **42 palms** total.

He notes that this calculation (*supputatio*) differs from an earlier computation cited from Jerome (*Hieronymus*), where no such ambiguity arose. The number 42 carries obvious Kabbalistic resonance — it is the length of the divine Name in the Kabbalistic tradition of the 42-letter Name (*Shem ha-Meforash* of 42 letters), a connection Zorzi likely intends to develop in subsequent *concentus*.

## Structural and Cosmological Significance

Zorzi frames Ezekiel's vision architecturally: the prophet begins with the divine tribunal (*divinum tribunal*) and ends with the measurements of what has been described, so that "the ultimate effects are to be joined and united with the first through those measurements." This circular structure — from divine judgment back through proportional measurement to its source — reflects Zorzi's Neoplatonic cosmology of emanation and return. The measurement of the temple is simultaneously a measurement of the soul's proportion to God (*hominem cum Deo proportionando metitur*).

## Editorial Notes and Sources

Campanini's footnotes (203, 204, 205, 206, 207) anchor the citations: the heading draws from Sirach 15:3 (water of wisdom); note 205 is Proverbs 1:20–21 (Wisdom in the streets); note 206 identifies the Hermetic Krater passage; note 207 is Lam. 4:8 combined with the living water imagery. The Latin and Italian columns run in parallel throughout, confirming the bilingual editorial apparatus of the Bompiani edition.

## Connection to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chunk demonstrates the characteristic Zorzian synthesis: Hermetic philosophy (the Krater as baptism into Nous), biblical prophecy (Proverbs, Lamentations, Ezekiel), Neoplatonic cosmology (the fountain of the Good and Beautiful), and proto-Kabbalistic numerology (the 42-palm reed) are woven into a single account of how divine Wisdom measures, structures, and animates both the cosmos and the soul. The pivot to Ezekiel's measurements signals that the *Cantico Terzo* is entering its most technically Kabbalistic phase, where prophetic architecture becomes a cipher for the hidden names and proportions of God.
